content.problemStatement:
Another practice problem for you!

Problem statementIn the gym, Chef prefers to lift at least WWW grams during a bench press and if that's impossible, Chef considers his workout to be incomplete and feels bad.

The rod weighs WrW_rWr​ grams and there are NNN other weights lying on the floor that weigh w1,w2,...,wNw_1, w_2, ..., w_Nw1​,w2​,...,wN​ grams. To maintain balance and to ensure that there is no unnecessary load due to torque, it's important that the weights added to the left side are symmetric to the weights added to the right side. It is not required to use all of the weights. It is also not required to use any weights at all, if Chef feels satisfied lifting only the rod.

For example:


111 222 222 111 ∣|∣Rod Center∣|∣ 111 111 111 333 is a wrong configuration, but
111 222 333 111 ∣|∣Rod Center∣|∣ 111 333 222 111 is a right configuration.
Find whether Chef will be able to collect the required weights to feel satisfied.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains two lines of input.
The first line contains three space-separated integers N,W,WrN, W, W_rN,W,Wr​.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers w1,w2,…,wNw_1, w_2, \ldots, w_Nw1​,w2​,…,wN​.


Output Format
For each test case, output the answer in a single line: "YES" if Chef will be satisfied after his workout and "NO" if not (without quotes).


You may print each character of each string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes" and "YES" will all be treated as identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains three space‑separated integers N, W and Wr – the number of available weights, the minimum total weight Chef wants to lift and the weight of the rod. The second line contains N space‑separated integers w1 … wN – the weights of the individual plates.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if Chef can achieve a total lifted weight of at least W grams (including the rod) while keeping the plates symmetric, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Rod weight 10 ≥ required 5 → satisfied.\nTest case 2: Only two 10‑gram plates can be paired, giving extra 40 grams; total 50+40=90 < 100 → not satisfied.\nTest case 3: Three pairs of 10‑gram plates give extra 60 grams; total 40+60=100 → satisfied.

hidden.label: general case where even best pairing is insufficient

solution.approach: For a symmetric configuration each plate used on the left must have an identical plate on the right. Therefore plates can only be taken in pairs of equal weight. For every distinct weight value v we can form floor(cnt[v]/2) pairs, each pair contributing 2·v grams to the total. The maximal extra weight obtainable is sum over all values of (cnt[v] - cnt[v] % 2) * v. If Wr alone already satisfies the requirement (Wr ≥ W) the answer is YES. Otherwise compute total = Wr + extra; if total ≥ W answer YES, else NO. The algorithm uses a hash map (or an array of size 10^5+1) to count frequencies, runs in O(N) per test case and O(N) additional memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of plates in that test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(K) where K is the number of distinct weight values (≤ 10^5).
